title: Foreign aid given per capita vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: [Net](#dod:net-oda) [official development assistance (ODA)](#dod:oda), expressed in US dollars and adjusted for inflation.
caption:
Foreign aid given per capita vs. GDP per capita
Net official development assistance (ODA), expressed in US dollars and adjusted for inflation.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between national wealth and foreign aid contributions, plotting GDP per capita in 2021 international dollars on the horizontal axis against net official development assistance given per capita in constant 2023 US dollars on the vertical axis. The data points, sized by population, reveal a general positive association where countries with higher GDP per capita tend to provide more aid per person, though the distribution shows significant variation. European nations dominate the upper portion of the chart, with Norway, Luxembourg, and Ireland positioned as outliers in the top right, each exceeding $450 in aid per capita while maintaining GDP per capita figures above $80,000. Switzerland and Sweden also appear in this high-contribution cluster, sitting between $400 and $500 per capita. In the middle range, the United Kingdom, Germany, and Japan form a grouping where GDP per capita hovers between $40,000 and $60,000 and aid contributions range from roughly $200 to $400 per person. The United States stands out as a distinct point with a large circle size indicating its massive population; it sits near $200 in aid per capita with a GDP per capita around $70,000. Asian economies like South Korea, Saudi Arabia, and Turkey cluster in the lower-left quadrant, showing GDP per capita between $30,000 and $60,000 and aid contributions generally under $200. Thailand and Kazakhstan appear near the bottom left with the lowest visible values on both axes. The visual arrangement confirms that while wealthier nations generally give more aid per person, the specific amounts vary widely even among countries with similar economic output.
